one_paragraph_summary: Panganiban opens by noting that Christmas and the New Year are grand occasions for family bonding and rejoicing â€” last Sunday he featured a sharing by grandson Miguel P. Sandejas (C99); today he presents an essay by another grandson, Rafa P. Yaptangco, a fourth-year student at Southridge School in Alabang submitted to secure college admission at Ateneo de Manila University. Rafa's essay begins: 'Ageless passion is what propelled my grandfather, retired Chief Justice Artemio V. Panganiban, in his rise from rags to riches, not in worldly wealth but in his multi-career fulfillment. His life story is so compelling and so moving that it, deservedly so, was brought to glory in a musicale for his 75th birthday celebration.' The opening message of the musicale comes from one of Lolo's favorite stories â€” about a farmer and his 20-year-old son experiencing good fortune, then bad fortune, then good fortune again, back and forth. The whole village would go to the farmer and marvel at his good fortune or show empathy at his bad fortune; and to everything, the farmer simply answered, 'Good news, bad news, who knows? I will just keep on doing my best and let God do the rest.' This has now become Rafa's mantra in life. Thinking back, Rafa never really appreciated who Lolo was until asked to represent the younger version of him in that musicale, titled 'Ageless Passion.' Cringing at fear of singing to a large audience, he politely turned down that opportunity and settled for a minor role; in hindsight he should have taken the challenge. During the experience he saw a different perspective he would not have been able to experience in his immediate society: poverty, both in the storyline and amongst the performers â€” most notably the choir, composed of 20 or so boys performing to raise funds for their school. The musicale tells of CJP's transformations: from newsboy from the backstreets of Sampaloc, Manila to the presidency of the Philippine Daily Inquirer; from an ignorant Catholic to an advisor to the Holy Father in the Vatican; from a frustrated applicant for graduate studies to father of five wonderful children who each achieved his impossible dream of graduating from a pedigreed US university; from a shoeshine boy to the boardrooms of the largest blue chips in our part of Planet Earth; and from a frustrated chemical engineer to a reluctant lawyer to the highest magistrate of the land. Rafa was taken aback at the story of Lolo's life â€” back then all he really thought of him was a caring father to his Mom who loved (and still does) to take the family out to Sunday dinners. Upon really understanding what Lolo has gone through, it absolutely amazed Rafa. It is incredibly hard to believe that all this was achieved by a single person â€” and it was done by Lolo. It was then Rafa realized what character he had and the ageless passion he has radiated; it was then Lolo became his idol, the person he hopes to eventually be like, the person for whom he has infinite respect. He shows that anyone willing to contribute a great deal of hard work for a better future can, indeed, have a better future. Rafa is extremely lucky to have been born into circumstances where finance is not a problem and infinitely grateful to be the grandson of a man so industrious. Following Lolo's life story, Rafa has set out with the general goal: to make Lolo proud. This threw life around, as he did not really have a life goal in mind at that time; to just be successful is not enough. Rafa now sees a light at the end of the tunnel: to employ the ageless passion Lolo has taught him through his life story. Even now, Lolo continues to work hard as he is in the board of numerous big-name companies; most of the things he talks about at the dinner table are about law and business â€” this just goes to show how dedicated to his work he is, and Rafa strives to someday achieve that. Rafa has gone on to cofound the Admirals Business Club of Southridge, managing numerous concerts including one to raise awareness for cancer patients in partnership with Moving as One (MAO), money earned given to charity. He has also cofounded LÃ©ger de Main, a cardistry, illusions, and deceptions club, teaching people card tricks and other forms of magic â€” hoping to instill values of confidence and composure in public performances. As leader he has driven both groups to be more unique organizations in Southridge, setting up a leadership-handoff system to ensure continuity post-graduation. In all this Rafa is thankful for Southridge's philosophy of forming men of integrity, grateful for his parents, older brother, relatives, and good friends â€” but most of all knows he must not stray from the valuable lesson of the farmer in Lolo's story. Already he can see himself on the right track to making Lolo proud. Obviously, it will not be a straight path to the goal; there will be highs and lows. 'Good news, bad news, who knows? I will just keep doing my best and let God do the rest' â€” circular bookend to the farmer-parable opening.
